
# System Design and Modelling {#sec-conception}

## Introduction

This chapter constitutes the core of the software engineering approach of the present
thesis. Drawing on the finding established in the previous chapter (the absence of a
dedicated digital solution for PFE management in the Algerian university context), the
task is now to translate this finding into a complete, structured, and rigorously specified
system design.

We will begin by defining the system requirements, then identify the actors involved and
associate each one with their specific functional needs. These needs will then be
formalised through a series of UML diagrams (use cases, classes, sequences, and activity
diagrams) in order to provide an unambiguous representation of the expected system
behaviour.

## Requirements Definition

The system to be developed must cover the entire lifecycle of a Final Year Project within
an Algerian university institution, taking the ESST in Algiers as the concrete use case.
The identified functionalities are as follows:

- **User management and authentication**: creation and maintenance of accounts (students,
  lecturers, administrators, companies), secure authentication, and role and permission
  management.
- **Academic parameter management**: administration of specialisations, research domains,
  cohorts, and academic years (submission periods, maximum number of allowed preferences).
- **PFE topic proposal and validation**: submission of topics by lecturers or partner
  companies, assignment of validators by the administration, and topic evaluation
  (validated, conditionally accepted, rejected).
- **Topic catalogue and expression of preferences**: consultation of validated topics by
  students, filtering by specialisation and domain, and expression of ranked preferences.
- **Application management**: consultation and processing of applications by topic
  proposers (acceptance or rejection of candidate students).
- **PFE assignment**: assignment of students to topics, generation of unique PFE codes
  (e.g. PFE-ISIL-2025-001), designation of the primary supervisor and co-supervisor where
  applicable.
- **Pedagogical supervision**: scheduling of progress meetings, writing of progress reports
  documenting the project's advancement (date, duration, meeting type, observations).
- **Thesis submission**: submission of the final thesis by the student before the set
  deadline.
- **Jury formation and defence scheduling**: selection of jury members (president and
  member), participation confirmation, and setting of the date, time, and room.
- **Grading and evaluation**: submission of grades by jury members (4 criteria, 4 points
  each) and by the supervisor (criterion 5, 4 points), calculation of the final grade out
  of 20 by the jury during deliberation.
- **Result determination**: attribution of the final result by the jury during deliberation
  (passed, corrections required with reservation statements, failed), archiving of validated
  PFE projects.
- **Partner company verification**: examination and validation of company registrations
  wishing to propose external topics.
- **Notifications**: automatic sending of notifications at critical stages (proposal,
  validation, assignment, scheduling, results).
- **Statistics and reports**: generation of dashboards, schedules, and statistical summaries
  for the administration.
- **Audit log**: complete traceability of sensitive actions with identification of the
  actor, timestamp, and associated metadata.

## System actors and functional requirements

The analysis of the PFE process at the ESST (cf. Chapter II) identified four main actors.
For each of them, we detail below the functional requirements that will be translated into
UML diagrams in the following section.

### The student

The student is the central actor of the PFE lifecycle. Their functional requirements are:

- Consult the catalogue of validated topics, filtered by specialisation and domain of
  interest.
- Express their preferences by ranking topics in order of preference, within the limit of
  the number of preferences allowed for the academic year.
- Consult their PFE assignment: assigned topic, PFE code, designated supervisors.
- Consult the progress reports filled in by their supervisor.
- Submit the final thesis before the set deadline.
- Consult results: defence schedule, jury composition, and final grade.

### The lecturer

The lecturer intervenes in several complementary roles. Their functional requirements are
grouped by role:

**As a topic proposer:**

- Propose PFE topics specifying the title, description, group type (individual, pair, or
  trio) and associated domains.
- Manage proposed topics and assigned topics.
- Consult the applications of students interested in their topics.
- Accept or reject candidate students.

**As a validator:**

- Evaluate topics proposed by other lecturers or companies when assigned as validator by
  the administration.
- Submit a decision (validated, conditionally accepted, rejected) accompanied by a
  justification comment.

**As a supervisor:**

- Provide pedagogical support to the student throughout the project.
- Fill in progress reports during regular meetings.
- Submit the supervisor's evaluation (criterion 5, graded out of 4 points).
- Report their availability or temporary unavailability.

**As a jury member:**

- Confirm participation in the defence jury (as president or member).
- Grade a PFE according to four evaluation criteria, each graded out of 4 points.

### The administration

The administration is the organisational and decision-making hub of the system. Their
functional requirements are:

- Manage users: create and maintain profiles for lecturers, students, companies, and
  administrators, including through batch import.
- Validate partner companies wishing to propose external topics.
- Assign two validator lecturers for each proposed PFE topic.
- Carry out PFE assignments taking into account preferences and available slots, generate
  the unique PFE code, and designate supervisors.
- Form defence juries by selecting the president and member from available lecturers.
- Schedule defences: set the date, time, and room.
- Manage academic parameters: specialisations, domains, cohorts, academic years.
- Consult statistics and export reports.

### The company

The partner company intervenes as an external proposer of PFE topics. Their functional
requirements are:

- Register on the platform and submit their profile for administrative verification.
- Propose PFE topics with a description of the professional context.
- Consult student applications for their topics.
- Accept or reject candidate students.
- Participate in PFE supervision through progress reports.
- Submit a correction report to the administration if needed.

## UML Modelling

The *Unified Modeling Language* (UML) is a graphical modelling language standardised by
the *Object Management Group* (OMG), used to specify, visualise, and document software
systems [@booch2005]. In its version 2.5, UML proposes thirteen types of diagrams
organised into two categories: structural diagrams (classes, objects, components) and
behavioural diagrams (use cases, sequence, activity) [@omg2017]. In the context of this
thesis, we use four types of diagrams: use cases to capture functional requirements, the
class diagram to model the business domain, sequence diagrams to describe interactions
between actors, the system, and the database, and activity diagrams to represent end-to-end
process flows.

### Use case diagrams

#### Student use cases

@fig-uc-etudiant represents all the functionalities accessible to the student. Their scope
is centred on browsing the catalogue, expressing preferences, monitoring their assignment,
and consulting results.

![Student use cases](/diagrams/uc-etudiant.png){#fig-uc-etudiant width="85%" fig-pos="H"}

#### Lecturer use cases

The lecturer intervenes through several roles: topic proposer, validator, supervisor, and
jury member. @fig-uc-enseignant covers all of these responsibilities. Application management
necessarily includes the prior proposal of a PFE topic (*include* relationship).

![Lecturer use cases](/diagrams/uc-enseignant.png){#fig-uc-enseignant width="85%" fig-pos="H"}

#### Administration use cases

The administration has the broadest functional scope, as illustrated by @fig-uc-admin.
It orchestrates the entire process: company validation, validator assignment, student
assignment, jury formation, and defence scheduling.

![Administration use cases](/diagrams/uc-administration.png){#fig-uc-admin width="85%" fig-pos="H"}

#### Company use cases

The partner company intervenes primarily in proposing external topics and monitoring the
PFE projects associated with it (@fig-uc-entreprise).

![Company use cases](/diagrams/uc-entreprise.png){#fig-uc-entreprise width="85%" fig-pos="H"}

### Class diagram

Figure `\ref{fig-dc-complet}`{=latex} presents the complete class diagram of the system.
It models all the domain entities: the hierarchy of user profiles (student, lecturer,
company), the academic context (academic year, cohort, specialisation, domain), PFE topics
and preferences, assignments and pedagogical supervision, as well as entities related to
evaluation (jury, defence, grades, and supervisor evaluation).

```{=latex}
\newgeometry{a2paper, landscape, margin=1cm}
\begin{landscape}
\thispagestyle{empty}
\centering
\vspace*{\fill}
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth,height=0.88\textheight,keepaspectratio]{diagrams/dc-classes-complet.png}
\captionof{figure}{System class diagram}
\label{fig-dc-complet}
\vspace*{\fill}
\end{landscape}
\restoregeometry
```

### Sequence diagrams

The sequence diagrams below illustrate the chronological interactions between the actor,
the system, and the DBMS for the most critical functional scenarios of the PFE process.

#### PFE topic proposal

@fig-seq-proposition describes the submission of a topic by a lecturer and its recording
in the database.

![Topic proposal](/diagrams/seq-proposition.png){#fig-seq-proposition width="90%" fig-pos="H"}

#### PFE topic validation

@fig-seq-validation describes the evaluation of a topic by an assigned validator, the
determination of the final status according to the decisions of both validators, and the
notification of the concerned student.

![Topic validation](/diagrams/seq-validation.png){#fig-seq-validation width="72%" fig-pos="H"}

#### Preferences and assignment

@fig-seq-voeux describes the student's journey from browsing the validated topic catalogue
to recording their preferences, with verification of the allowed limit.

![Preferences and assignment](/diagrams/seq-voeux-affectation.png){#fig-seq-voeux width="80%" fig-pos="H"}

#### Jury formation and scheduling

@fig-seq-jury describes the formation of the jury by the administration and the logistical
scheduling of the defence.

![Jury formation and scheduling](/diagrams/seq-jury-planification.png){#fig-seq-jury width="80%" fig-pos="H"}

#### Grading

@fig-seq-notation illustrates the submission of grades by jury members and the supervisor,
followed by the automatic calculation of the final grade out of 20 by the system during
deliberation.

![Grading](/diagrams/seq-notation.png){#fig-seq-notation width="82%" fig-pos="H"}

#### Results

@fig-seq-resultats describes the determination of the final result by the jury president
during deliberation, according to three possible outcomes: passed, corrections required
(with reservation statements), or failed.

![Results](/diagrams/seq-resultats.png){#fig-seq-resultats width="70%" fig-pos="H"}

### Activity diagrams

The activity diagrams below represent the end-to-end flow of the PFE process, split into
two phases for readability.

Figure `\ref{fig-act-proposition}`{=latex} covers the first half of the process: topic
proposal, validation (including the case of conditional acceptance with clearance of
conditions before confirmation), publication to the catalogue, expression of preferences
by students, candidate selection, and administrative assignment.

@fig-act-suivi covers the second half: project work, pedagogical supervision, thesis
submission, jury formation, defence, and deliberation during which the jury calculates the
final grade and determines the result (passed, corrections required with reservation
statements, or failed).

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth,height=0.70\textheight,keepaspectratio]{diagrams/act-proposition-affectation.png}
\caption{Activity - From proposal to assignment}
\label{fig-act-proposition}
\end{figure}
```

![Activity - From supervision to results](/diagrams/act-suivi-resultats.png){#fig-act-suivi width="95%" fig-pos="H"}

## Conclusion

This chapter laid the formal foundations of the proposed application solution. Starting
from a precise identification of the four system actors (student, lecturer, administration,
and company), we defined a set of business processes covering the entire PFE lifecycle:
topic proposal, peer validation, expression of preferences, assignment, pedagogical
supervision, thesis submission, jury formation, defence, and grading according to a
five-criterion rubric out of 20 points.

The resulting UML modelling (use cases, classes, sequences, and activity diagrams)
provides an unambiguous specification of the expected system behaviour, constituting both
a contract between stakeholders and a roadmap for the implementation phase.

The following chapter will focus on translating this specification into a concrete
application, detailing the technology choices and the developed interfaces.
